title: 2019 Dean's Report
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
January 28, 2019 | "2019 Dean's Report"

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon falls on the Fourth Sunday after the Epiphany in the Christian calendar, positioned in Ordinary Time following the Christmas/Epiphany season. Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings likely included 1 Corinthians 1:18-31 and Matthew 4:12-23, focusing on Paul's discussion of God's wisdom and Jesus calling his first disciples. The liturgical mood would be one of gentle transition back into the ordinary rhythms of the church year after the festive post-Christmas season.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the early months following the 2018 General Convention (held in Austin, Texas in July), which had approved resolutions on immigration, gun violence, and inclusion of LGBTQ+ persons. The Diocese of Colorado was in relative stability under Bishop Robert O'Neill, who had been in office since 2005. January 2019 represented no major diocesan crisis or transition, allowing for standard institutional reporting and reflection in the Dean's Report format.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced typical winter weather patterns in late January with below-freezing temperatures and periodic snow. The Colorado legislature had just reconvened on January 9, 2019 for its 120-day session, with Democratic majorities in both chambers focusing on healthcare expansion, education funding, and climate policy. The Cathedral's Capitol Hill location meant proximity to legislative activity, and Denver's robust downtown revitalization continued with ongoing construction and development projects.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The partial federal government shutdown had ended on January 25, 2019, just three days before this sermon, after 35 days of closure over border wall funding disputes—a significant national stress point that affected federal employees nationwide. The 2020 presidential campaign was beginning to crystallize with early Democratic primary candidates announcing or preparing to announce. The #MeToo movement and questions about sexual misconduct in institutions remained prominent national conversation topics.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Venezuelan political crisis had intensified dramatically, with opposition leader Juan Guaidó challenging Nicolás Maduro's authority in early February (imminent at this date), creating a major hemispheric diplomatic situation. The British government was navigating the final stages of Brexit negotiations with the EU ahead of the March 29 deadline, generating significant international uncertainty. North Korea continued diplomatic engagement with the United States following 2018 talks, though conditions remained fragile.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood in late January 2019 reflected deep partisan polarization following the government shutdown and ongoing Mueller investigation into the Trump campaign. However, there was also a sense of tentative optimism as the immediate shutdown crisis resolved and winter moved toward spring. The Denver Broncos had just completed a disappointing 6-10 season, while attention turned toward the Super Bowl (February 3, 2019), creating typical late-January sports focus; in the broader cultural moment, streaming services were increasingly dominant, and the #TimesUp movement continued reshaping Hollywood and workplace norms.
